title: Quick check: Applying Tesla thinking elsewhere

questions.prompt: Which of these Tesla manufacturing principles transfers most cleanly to a software company?

choices.text: Software-defined operations as the discipline of instrumentation, dashboards, and version-controlled deploys. In a software company this becomes CI/CD, observability stacks, DORA metrics, and SRE practice. Different domain, same underlying loop: observe → hypothesize → change → deploy → measure → keep or revert. Other Tesla tactics (gigacasting, vertical battery integration) are physical-product specific and don't translate.

questions.explanation: The operational discipline transfers; the physical tactics don't. SRE and DevOps are essentially manufacturing reliability and continuous improvement applied to services. The Tesla loop is recognizable inside every well-run engineering org.

questions.prompt: A modular-housing startup wants to copy Tesla's vertical integration play and bring lumber milling, panel assembly, plumbing pre-fab, and on-site installation all in-house from launch. Smart?

choices.text: Likely a Katerra repeat. Vertical integration is capital-intensive and only viable when you have product-market fit, deep funding access, and a clear strategic reason per integration. A new modular-housing startup should validate demand and prove operations on one or two integrations at a time, buy commodities, and partner where suppliers are already efficient. Copying the entire Tesla playbook from day one without Tesla's capital base or product-market fit is the failure pattern, not the success pattern.

questions.explanation: Katerra burned roughly $2B applying vertical-integration ambition to construction without sustainable funding or product-market fit. Copy Tesla's reasoning ('own what controls quality, cost, cycle time') one integration at a time, not the entire playbook at once.
